titleStmt.title: Letter from John Chamberlain to Dudley Carleton, 20 December 1598

publicationStmt.p: Published digitally at earlymodernletters.org. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

div.p: The world is litle or nothing chaunged here since I wrote last, only the Quene is resolved to kepe Christmas at Whitehall, and the matters of Ireland stand at a stay or rather go backward, for the erle of Essex his jorny thether that was in suspence is now they say quite dasht — not that there is any other order taken, but only that things shall still drive on there as they may, and perhaps they hope cunctando restitutere rem,An allusion to the Roman dictator Quintus Fabius Maximus, whose delaying tactics against Hannibal gave rise to the motto cunctando restituit rem — "by delaying he restored the commonwealth." Chamberlain deploys it sardonically: Fabian delay may suit some crises, but not, he implies, deplorata valetudine — a condition already desperate. which all men take to be a wronge course in deplorata valetudine and so desperate a disease. From Tuesday last till Sunday yt held fast and firm that the erle of Essex was to go, and all things were accordingly setled and set downe, but a sodain alteration came on Sunday night the reason wherof is yet kept secret. Some say the Quene had promised to forgeve him twelve thowsand pound debt due by his father, and 20000li he ought her himself for cochenillaCochineal, a red dye imported from Spanish America and highly prized in the Elizabethan textile trade. Essex apparently owed the crown a large sum arising from a licence or transaction in cochineal since his last journey — possibly the Islands Voyage of 1597. since his last jorny, which belike was mistaken, for the Quene sayes she meant but the forbearance of yt, and that yt shold not be called upon in his absence; but whether yt were this or some other matter all is turnd upside downe. He and Master Secretarie have so goode leysure that they plie the tables hard in the presence chamber and play so round game as yf Ireland were to be recovered at Irish.Irish was a board game resembling backgammon. The image of Essex and Cecil gambling at court while Ireland burns is one of Chamberlain's sharpest ironies. The rebells in the mean time have don many outragious and slaine lately Sir Tho. MooreSir Thomas Moore, nicknamed "the lying knight," an English settler or officer in Ireland killed by the rebels in late 1598. The mocking sobriquet is unusual; Chamberlain seems to relay it with some relish. (surnamed the lieng knight) and his wife that was Sir Henry Dukes daughter The wife of Sir Thomas Moore, daughter of Sir Henry Duke; Chamberlain describes her as "the properest gentlewoman of Ireland that I saw," suggesting he had met her personally at some point. and the properest gentlewoman of Ireland that I saw, and hurt Cap. Gifford, and 800 men went to scowre the country about Dublin. They have likewise overthrowne or defaced fowre or five strong places or castles as they call them. Sir Richard Bingham with twenty went to the Nasse, which is not past ten or twelve miles from Dublin, but before he came thither he was so incountred and beset that he was fain to retire without attempting further, and fell sicke at Dublin, where some say he is dead.

Doctor Parkins at his first comming out of Denmarke made his bragges that he had brought matters there to a goode passe, but all he hath don is only a promise of 40000 dollers to be restored on condition his subjects may be satisfied for such injuries as they have receved, and to that purpose he sent an ambassador along with him who had audience on Sunday last, but his message was nothing pleasing, being for the most part a complaint of our piracies. And as Dr. Parkins returned with half an aunswer, so they say Master CaryGeorge Cary, sent on an embassy to Poland; he returned having failed to secure any audience with the Polish court. Chamberlain treats the paired failures of the Danish and Polish missions as symptomatic of the general mismanagement of English affairs abroad. is come out of Poland with none at all, nor could get no audience. The ships that were staide by Sir Richard Lewson stole away most of them the night following, and left him con tanto di nasoItalian idiom: literally "with so much nose," meaning left looking foolish — equivalent to the English "left holding the bag." The remark extends Chamberlain's caustic survey of English incompetence at home and abroad. as the Italian saith, so sutable is the managing of our affaires both at home and abrode. Fowre or five shippes of your Dunkerke neighbours gave a great alarme lately to Yorkeshire and made them fire theyre beacons, but that was all the harme they did; yt is thought they meant to have landed and taken some prisoners to help to redeem theyre governor.Don Francisco de Aquillar Alverado, Governor of Dunkirk, had been a prisoner at Ostend since 1597 — the very garrison where Carleton was serving. Chamberlain's aside "your Dunkerke neighbours" acknowledges this local detail. We heare the States make huge levies of mony and meane to be masters of the feild the next sommer whatsoever yt cost them. The French king is sending to the Pope to determine the controversie of the Marquisat of Saluces,The Marquisate of Saluzzo (Saluces), an Alpine territory taken by Savoy from France in 1588. Henri IV was pressing for its return or equivalent compensation; the matter was eventually settled by the Treaty of Lyon in 1601. for that in the treaty of peace that was referred to his arbitrement. The loane of 150000li makes many of our citizens shrincke and pull in theyre hornes, but yet yt goes forward and they must furnish yt; and some whispering there is that when that is don we shall heare of a benevolence.A benevolence was a "voluntary" gift exacted by the crown from wealthy subjects — technically a free gift, in practice a forced levy. The rumour of one coming on top of the current loan reflects Chamberlain's dim view of royal fiscal management. Yf we be driven to these shifts alredy God knowes how we shall hold hereafter, and I marvayle that they which knew these wants did hearken no more after the peace when they might have had yt with goode conditions.

You see how confidently I write to you of all things, but I hope you kepe yt to yourself, and then there is no daunger; and I am so used to a libertie and fredome of speach when I converse or write to my frends that I cannot easilie leave yt. Your brother and sister Williams marvaile they heare not from you. I have had much ado to excuse myself this Christmas from KnebworthThe Hertfordshire seat of Rowland Lytton, where Chamberlain often stayed. and Ascot,The Oxfordshire house of Michael Dormer. but specially from Knebworth, the rather because Wat CopeWalter Cope (c. 1553–1614), administrator and collector; secretary to Robert Cecil and later keeper of Whitehall Palace. He assembled a famous cabinet of curiosities in his house on Kensington High Street. Knighted in 1603. and his wife, Hugh Beeston, and Master Carew go thether; but upon some occasions I am growne so privat that I stirre not abrode, nor mean to do but to live at home like a snaile in the shell. And so, wishing you a goode new yeare and many, I end.

postscript.p: To my very goode frend Master Dudley Carleton attending on the Lord Governor of Ostende geve these.
